In other words, there's a lot of dividend growth potential, which comes with aggressive buybacks, as URI has bought back 9% of its shares over the past three years alone.

Although the current environment is tough, I believe URI is a critical part of Trump's plan to revive American manufacturing and broaden economic growth.

Especially in light of data center growth, pipeline shortages, factory construction, and other large projects, URI has a front seat in America's economy.

Trading at 13.4x earnings, the company is expected to grow its EPS by 11-12% in 2026 and 2027.

While these numbers are subject to change (now more than ever), I am very bullish on URI and would be a buyer if I did not already own Caterpillar (CAT), which is a correlated play.

Takeaway

Last week was one for the history books, as we saw brutal volatility, policy chaos, and a lot of fear.

I can't say with certainty whether we've seen the bottom, but I can say this: the setup increasingly looks like an event-driven correction, not a prolonged downturn.

Valuations in some corners of the market, especially mid-caps and value stocks, are attractive, sentiment is bearish (which is good), and policy risks might actually become tailwinds.

That's why I'm using this environment to buy quality stocks that bring something special to the table.

In this article, we discussed three of them. One with a high yield and two with low yields but aggressive growth.

Going forward, I expect all of these stocks to beat the market and deliver consistent dividend growth.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk to my thesis is prolonged policy uncertainty.

If tariff tensions escalate further or if the Federal Reserve has to delay rate cuts due to sticky inflation, market volatility could remain elevated and pressure the stock market.

data.title: Seritage Growth Properties: Special Situation Buy
data.description:
Summary

Seritage Growth Properties (NYSE:SRG) was spun off from Sears Holdings back in 2015. Currently, it is in the liquidation stage. Shareholders have approved a Plan of Sale to dispose all of its assets back in October 2022. This has transformed SRG into a liquidating trust.

Investment Thesis

I believe that the market is overbearish on SRG, creating a potential mispricing. From my calculation, the asset value actually exceeds its current market capitalization using a NAV calculation and deducting its credit obligations. Essentially, the market is valuing SRG at a discount relative to its estimated net asset value. Of course, this is dependent on the management's execution of the Plan of Sale. If they are able to sell the remaining properties quickly, the IRR would be higher to shareholders.

However, this investment is without risk as it has a negative carry. It is a company that is slowly bleeding. SRG is cash burning as well. The company reported negative profit of $154 million in 2024. This is mainly driven by lower rental revenue compared to its high cost. Moreover, its maturity of debt is approaching next year, which also caused the heavy discount.

What is driving the mispricing?

Currently, SRG has a market capitalization of ~$150M, and is trading at 0.37x P/B. There are a few factors driving the mispricing:

Poor Outlook

SRG's poor financials are hard to ignore. Its earnings and cash are diminishing. Moreover, funds are also forced to sell the small-cap due to either mandates and its unattractive quality of a REIT with no distributions and a finite lifespan. Management also warned market headwinds, which led to investors taking a walk away from this investment. However, I believe, this selling pressure causes the share price to fall more than it should.

Hard Deadlines

July 2025 is the debt maturity, and this created fear to investors. However, management has negotiated an extension of its term loan to July 2026. However, this scenario would be insufficient for risk-averse investors, which led to this strong pessimism.

Insider Alignment

Eddie Lampert, the former Sears CEO, still owns a good ~24% of SRG shares. We can still take some comfort that they would act in their own favor to sell these assets and maximize the remaining value. Therefore, the market seems to be fearful and for good reasons. However, a combination of the aforementioned factors and a deeper dive into the properties would paint a story that is worth looking into more.

NAV Analysis

As of 2024, SRG's portfolio has a cumulative 17 properties with ownership of ~1.3 million sq ft, sitting on 220 acres of land. The properties are split into 10 wholly owned (~0.9 million sq ft., 166 acres) and 7 joint ventures (~0.8 million sq. ft., 108 acres).

SRG's real estate portfolio comprises both wholly owned properties and joint venture (JV) assets. Wholly owned properties are fully controlled and operated, meaning 100% of their rental income, expenses, and sale proceeds accrue directly to the company. In contrast, joint venture properties are co-owned with third-party partners such as Simon Property Group or Howard Hughes Corporation.

In order to derive an implied valuation of the properties, I did a bottom-up perspective with various assumptions. A few variables that have to be figured out are: (1) Rent/Square Feet, (2) Fair Expense Ratio, and (3) Cap Rate. Once we have these 3 figures across each property, we can then find out the fair value of each property in a rough sense. Do note that the properties are valued in a context where, should the properties are running in a normalized condition (not in a liquidation), how much would the properties be worth.

Rent per Square Foot (rent/sf) for each SRG property was assigned based on a combination of market comparables and property quality and parts of company disclosure. As the company did not do a full disclosure for each property, the best guess is to use logic and market comps to derive them. Looking at SRG's recent lease rates for one of its properties reaching $56/sf and its disclosure range of rent/sf to be between $20 and $58. Therefore, my assumptions would be within this range. Fully leased assets and great locations such as Braintree, San Diego, and Redmond are assigned as $40/sf. However, properties like Aventura and King of Prussia, are mid-tier with partial leasing and strong suburban fundamentals, I assigned $30-37/sf. Weaker properties, such as Clearwater and Altamonte Springs, were assigned $25-30/sf. Thus, this is accounting for tenant quality and occupancy status.

As stated above, the expense ratio is set at 30% to reflect normalized operating margin. In this context, a 30% fair expense ratio is used, which implies a 70% NOI margin. Looking at peers like Regency Center Corporation and Federal Realty Investment Trust, their estimated NOI margins are around ~70%. This means that property-level operating expenses typically fall around 30% of gross rent.

Lastly, the cap rates are assigned similarly with similar methodology as rent/sf based on its riskiness, and quality of properties. This means that a property that is fully leased deserves a lower cap rate, as it would imply a higher valuation. The valuation range is 6.5%-8.5%. Cap rates of 6.5-7.0% are given to stabilized, income-producing properties. 7.5-8.5% are for partial leasing. These figures are anchored based on a disclosure by SRG in Q1 2025, where 7.7% cap rate was disclosed in SRG's own transaction data.

Calculation of NAV

Based on the calculation above, we are getting $282 million for just properties alone. This is lower than its book value of $358 million, which makes sense as it accounts for non-revenue generating assets and conservative purposes. Then we can add the cash of $97.5 million and land of $65 million, and we treat the rest as unclaimable for conservative stake. This gives us a total of ~$445 million ($65 million + $282 million + $97.5 million) as implied valuation. Then we minus total liabilities of $271 million, giving us a net asset value of $174 million. Currently, SRG's market cap is $150 million. This means that there is a potential ~16% return of value that the market is mispricing. This gives us an NAV of $3/share ($174 million / 56.255 million in shares outstanding).

Asymmetric Risk/Reward

SRG's deep decline has already priced in substantial bad news, and any positive could swing the share price upwards. Assuming a timeline of roughly 1 year for the liquidation to play out (mid to end-2026) as that is where its term loan maturity hits.

In the Bull Case, NAV $7/share. This is achieved assuming the financials in SRG's are fairly valued, and this would give us a total asset of $674 million with total liabilities of $272 million. The net asset would be $402 million ($674 million - $272 million), deriving ~$7/share. That is a ~160% gain. But the assumptions are unlikely to hit unless the stars are fully aligned where maturity is extended again and interest rates fall quicker than expected, giving these properties room to transform from a liquidating trust into an operating business. However, I know that the probability of hitting this is pretty unlikely to hit.

As for the base case, NAV is $3/share. This means that investing would see a 17% gain in one year, which is not bad. The base case essentially suggests you won't lose money and could make a modest return. If SRG achieves a fair liquidation, and assuming all the assumptions above play out in the calculation of NAV, hitting $3/share can be expected.

Lastly, a bear case would be actually $0/share. If the worst happens when the properties are not able to be sold out until the maturity happens, common shareholders get nothing at all. So, the downside is essentially losing your entire investment. But of this, this is dependent that the management is unable to liquidate these properties, which, I believe, is also quite unlikely.

Conclusion

As, this particular investment idea is definitely more speculative than my other articles because the nature of this investment is both on balance sheet investing basis and dependent on management's ability to sell these properties before the maturity hits. But do also note that my assumptions of getting these properties fair value are pretty conservative, as the rent/sf is not aggressive at all.

You can also think of it as the market is pricing this investment like an out-of-the-money call option, but in actuality it is an in-the-money as the assets are worth more than the market capitalization. Lastly, insider ownership is still quite high at 24%, and I don't think the management have any plans to give up on them.

data.title: Greenback Remains On The Defensive, While Stocks And Bonds Come Back Better Bid
data.description:
Overview

It seems the dramatic reaction in the capital markets and the collapse of consumer confidence (and erosion of public support for its policies) prompted the Trump administration to postpone the reciprocal tariffs. And after the markets closed Friday, it announced on its website that smartphones, computers and other popular consumer electronics, and machines used to fabricate semiconductors were exempt from the so-called reciprocal tariffs and should have been part of the April 2 announcement. The administration was quick to say that coming tariffs on semiconductors, which it knows are coming even though the investigation is not completed, will cover the consumer electronic products. An announcement on semiconductor tariffs is expected soon. The repeated adjustments seem to reduce the credibility of claims of some "master plan" unfolding.

Comparisons of the US with emerging markets in recent days seems nearly as extreme as the epithets against the UK during the short-lived Truss affair in September 2022. While it helped us call sterling's low, the dollar's downtrend is intact, but the firmer tone in US index futures and bonds has removed some of the last week's manic feel. Asia Pacific markets rallied today after a three-week, 8.5% slide. Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is up 2% today after falling 11.5% in its three-week drop. Benchmark 10-year yields in Europe mostly 3-7 bps lower and peripheral premiums are narrowing. UK Gilts are outperforming with a nearly nine basis point decline. The 10-year US Treasury (US10Y) reached almost 4.60% before the weekend and is slipping below 4.44%. Gold is consolidating near the record high set before the weekend, slightly below $3246. May WTI is firm near $62 as prices continue to consolidate with last Thursday's range (~$58.75-$63.35).

USD: The Dollar Index recovered from the spike down to almost 99.00 in early European trading before the weekend, and in the volatile session, met sellers on the move to almost 100.40. The session high was near 100.75 in Asia Pacific turnover. US equities recovered and yields pulled back but only after the 10-year made new highs for the move near 4.59%. It has remained below Friday's settlement (~110.10) today but also above 99.20. With US bonds and stocks trading higher, the tone is less manic. After last week's inflation gauges, attention turns back to the real sector. Retail sales, industrial production, and housing starts are featured. The high frequency data, however, seems less significant now for at least two reasons. First, some will be flattered, like retail sales (helped by stronger than expected auto sales) and some data will not show the impact of the headwind coming to the US. Bloomberg estimated that despite the postponement of the reciprocal tariffs, because of the hike on China, the average effective tariff is down from 27% but at 24% it still poses a threat to growth and prices.

Euro: Before the weekend, the euro settled a little above the middle of the day's range and still finished almost 1.4% higher and rallying 2.3% the day before. The euro reached almost $1.1475, its highest level in three years, in late Asia/early European activity following China's announcement of another (and it says last) increase in its tariff on the US. The euro was capped near $1.1410 in North America and spent most of the afternoon straddling the $1.13 area. The weekly gain of more than 3.5% was also the largest weekly advance since November 2022. Today, it saw the session low early near $1.11280 and reached $1.1425 as European markets were opening. It looks poised to challenge the pre-weekend high. The eurozone sees the aggregate February industrial production figures and Germany's ZEW survey tomorrow, but the highlight of the week ECB meeting on Thursday. There was some debate about an April cut, but the market has clearly become more confident of a quarter-point rate cut. The growth impact from the tariffs, and the price implications of the euro's strength and the drop in oil prices, encourages such views. In early March, the swaps market was discounting a little more than 40 bp of cuts in the remainder of the year. Now, a little more than 75 bps is priced into the swaps curve.

CNY: Rather than a sharp devaluation of the yuan by Beijing in response to the tariffs, it is the dollar that fell. In the past three sessions, the dollar fell by about 2% against the offshore yuan. Ahead of the weekend, the dollar fell to a six-day low against the offshore yuan. And in the face of the escalation of the tariff war, the offshore yuan rose for the first time in four weeks. Its greenback is snapping the three-day decline and is up almost 0.3% to CNH7.31 in the European morning have been up to almost CNH7.3175. The PBOC has not been taken off its course (relatively stable yuan against the dollar). Since Trump's inauguration, the onshore and offshore yuan have depreciated by about 0.40% and 0.60%, respectively. We suspect when push comes to shove, Beijing will choose more domestic stimulus over a large devaluation against the dollar to blunt the impact of the tariffs. The PBOC set the dollar's fix at CNY7.2100 (CNY7.2087 before the weekend, which was the first lower dollar fix in seven sessions). March trade surplus was larger than expected at $102.6 bln, up from $58.6 bln in March 2024. China's trade dominance has increased since Trump's initial tariffs in 2018, but today's figures were likely bolstered by attempts from exporters and importers to get ahead of the tariffs. Exports are up about 12.4% year-over-year, while imports are 4.3% lower. Aggregate financing jumped more than expected in March, reaching CNH15.18 trillion, year-to-date, compared with CNY12.8 trillion as of March 2024. However, while bank lending increased, the lion's share of the improvement came from the so-called shadow banking (e.g., non-bank financial institutions). Bank lending increased by around CNY222 bln of the increase from March 2024, while the shadow banking accounted for the remainder.

JPY: In the past three sessions, the dollar fell nearly 4.2% against the Japanese yen to approach JPY142.00. After retesting the low in early North American trade, short covering helped lift the greenback to almost JPY144.20, where sellers pushed it back toward JPY143.60. The dollar is trading within the pre-weekend range today. A break of JPY142 may require a further pullback in US yields, even though the volatility of US rates and the dollar appears to have loosened the relationship between the US 10-year yield and the exchange rate. Our working hypothesis is that it may take a bigger increase in the US premium to compensate investors. There is much talk that Japanese investors have been selling Treasuries. It is possible, but where can it be documented? Capital flows are reported with a lag. It is a bit like US jobs. The media reports large layoffs, not the hiring. Market scuttlebutt is about Japanese selling, but they do that every day, but there are also Japanese buyers. One of the most authoritative sources of Japanese purchases of foreign bonds reported last week that they were net buyers in February of about $9.6 bln of US bonds, which hardly sheds light on recent activity.

GBP: Sterling is extended last week's gain to approach $1.3200 after it reached $1.3145 last Friday. It had spent most of the pre-weekend session and spent most of the European session and the entire North American session consolidating in choppy activity. It was unable to rise much above $1.3130 in North America and recorded a low about a cent lower shortly after Europe closed the books them for the week. Still, a higher close today allows sterling to extend its advance for the fifth consecutive session, the longest run since last November. The UK employment report is due tomorrow, and the March CPI is on Wednesday. Barring a significant surprise, the market's confidence of a rate cut at the Bank of England's May 8 meeting. It is fully discounted. More telling of the shift in market sentiment is the extent of rate cuts now anticipated for this year. As recently as March 25, the swaps market has about 40 bp of cuts discounted, which is one quarter-point cut and a 60% of second. Now, there are nearly 90 bp of cuts priced into the swaps curve. That puts the year-end rate near 3.55% from over 4% in late March.

CAD: The US dollar last traded above CAD1.44 on April 1 and ahead of the weekend fell to CAD1.3840, its lowest level since last November. It has made a marginal new low today near CAD1.3830. For the third consecutive session, the greenback settled below its lower Bollinger Band, which is found around CAD1.3910 today. Chart support may be around CAD1.3800. Canada reports its March CPI tomorrow. The Bank of Canada meets Wednesday. Through last month, the central bank slashed its policy rate starting June 2024 from 5.0% to 2.75%. The market flirted with the idea of another cut this week. The odds a week ago were near 66% and have been halved, even though one might think the strength of the Canadian dollar (the best level since last October) may encourage a pre-cautionary cut given the likely tariff headwind. Still, proximity of the April 28 election may be a consideration.

AUD: The Australian dollar posted a key upside reversal in the middle of last week by making a new (five-year) low and then recovered to settle above the previous day's high. Follow-through buying lifted the Aussie to $0.6300 ahead of the weekend; a 6.5% rally from Wednesday's low (~$0.5915). The rally has been extended to almost $0.6345 today. The risk-reward changes as the top of the four-month range is approached around $0.6400. The Reserve Bank of Australia's minutes from the meeting earlier this month may draw some passing interesting tomorrow, but the importance will be in the clues to the bar for next month's meeting. The futures market is getting aggressive. It is discounting nearly a 40% chance of a 50 bp cut. It is now pricing almost 120 bp of cuts this year. That is nearly twice as much as was being discounted as recently as March 27.

MXN: The greenback snapped a three-week advance against the peso last week and fell about 0.60%. The dollar posted a bearish key reversal in the middle of last week, but there was no follow-through selling. It ended up consolidating for the past two sessions. It is trading with a heavier bias today. A break of MXN20.17 is needed to open up the downside and spur a test to MXN20.00. The month's low was set April 3 near MXN19.84, which at the time was a little above the 200-day moving average, which comes in now slightly below MXN19.88.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
